mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender P300 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned P300 engine typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only. Fitting adds £700–£1,100 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £1,500–£2,800, but condition and warranty vary. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Defender.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender P300 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Generally, yes — the 204PT petrol Ingenium is considered more reliable than its diesel counterpart. There's no timing chain tensioner catastrophe. The main issues are cooling system-related: water pump failure and coolant leaks. With proper maintenance, it's a robust and refined engine for the modern Defender.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Defender P300?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are water pump failure (plastic impeller and seal degradation), coolant leaks from plastic hoses and thermostat housings, and occasional high-pressure fuel pump wear. None are catastrophic if caught early — regular coolant system inspections are key.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender P300 engine, or should I scrap the vehicle?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the high retained value of the L663 Defender (typical £30,000+ for a good example), a £4,000–£5,000 engine replacement is comfortably within the 'worth it' range. Our Land Rover scrap threshold is 60% of vehicle value — with a £4,000 engine cost against a £30,000 vehicle, it's a clear yes.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Defender P300 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 204PT, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 2.0-litre turbocharged petrol Ingenium engine. If you're unsure, enter your registration on our site and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a P300 water pump, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The pump must be replaced — it's a sealed unit with plastic impellers that degrade. A preventative replacement at 60,000 miles or at the first sign of leakage costs £400–£800. Ignoring it can lead to overheating and head gasket failure, which is far more expensive.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Defender P300 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Replace pump; no other damage if caught early
Worth it on a £30k+ Defender if the rest is sound
Replacement cost may be justified only on high-value examples

buyingChecks.title: Buying This P300? Check These First
buyIf: * Full JLR service history with documented coolant system inspections
* No signs of coolant loss or overheating (check coolant reservoir level)
* Water pump replaced as preventative at 60k+ miles
* A 2020–present L663 Defender — the modern platform with the robust petrol Ingenium
avoidIf: * Unexplained coolant loss with no visible leak — could indicate early water pump or head gasket issues
* Overheating history or low coolant level — risk of engine damage
* Hard starting or rough idle — may indicate HPFP or injector problems
* Dealer-serviced but with no records of coolant system work — assume it's overdue

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The P300 is the petrol alternative to the diesel Ingenium in the L663 Defender. It's generally more reliable than the diesel — no timing chain catastrophe — but the cooling system requires vigilance. Water pump and coolant leaks are the main issues; catch them early and the 204PT is a strong, refined engine for the modern Defender.

cta.label: → Compare P300 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Defender P300 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for smooth idle; any hesitation or rough running points to injector or fuel pump issues
* Service History — Including coolant system inspections and fuel filter changes — see the map above
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss is a red flag
* Water Pump — Listen for whining noise; check for coolant residue near the pump area
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any lack of power or hesitation?
* Oil Condition — Check for fuel smell (HPFP wear) or coolant contamination
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate HPFP or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (though petrol is generally clean)
